step_by_step_reasoning: Analyze the whiteboard architecture and transcript to identify the three main layers: Process, Store, and Consume. We map the external vehicle sources (left) to a landing S3 bucket via a REST API, which triggers SQS queues processed by EKS. Processed data is sent to S3 and orchestrated by Step Functions, then stored in Elasticsearch and CockroachDB. Finally, the consume layer exposes REST APIs via API Gateway and a Web App backed by another EKS cluster, serving external city planners and operators.

graph.name: Mobileye Dynamic RAM Platform
graph.notes: Dynamic RAM is a multi-layer mobility intelligence platform processing vehicle telemetry to provide city planners with road and traffic insights.

nodes.name: Vehicles
nodes.notes: Anonymized telemetry and visual inspections sent from cars.

nodes.name: REST API
nodes.notes: Ingestion REST API in a separate AWS account.

nodes.name: SQS Queue
nodes.notes: Buffers raw messages for scaling downstream processing.

nodes.name: Processing EKS
nodes.notes: Autoscaling workers that process vehicle data.

nodes.name: Step Functions
nodes.notes: Orchestrates algorithm flows and Lambda functions.

nodes.name: Elasticsearch
nodes.notes: Document store for quick queries and self-indexing.

nodes.name: CockroachDB
nodes.notes: Relational DB running on EKS for frequent updates.

nodes.name: API Gateway
nodes.notes: Exposes secure REST APIs to consumers.

nodes.name: Consume EKS
nodes.notes: EKS cluster serving the frontend and REST APIs.

nodes.name: City Planners
nodes.notes: Consumes REST APIs for road segment and traffic insights.

nodes.name: End Users
nodes.notes: Users accessing the web application dashboard.
